result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
    actor Attacker
    participant EK as process_encK
    participant SK as process_signK
    participant S as processS
    participant Init as processInitiator (xA = B)
    participant Resp as processResponder (xB = B)

    alt secretA attack
        Attacker->>EK: register (B, pk(attacker_sk))
        Attacker->>S: request certs for (B, B)
        S-->>Attacker: cert1 = sign((B, spkB), skS)
        S-->>Attacker: cert2 = sign((B, pk(attacker_sk)), skS)
        Attacker->>Init: start with xA = B
        Attacker->>Init: send cert1, cert2
        Init-->>Attacker: cert1, cert2, encrypt(sign((B, B, k), sskB), pk(attacker_sk))
        Note over Attacker: decrypts message, extracts k
        Init-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretA, k)
        Note over Attacker: decrypts and learns secretA
    else secretB attack
        Attacker->>SK: register (fakeHost, fakeSpk)
        Attacker->>EK: register (A, attacker_controlled_key)
        Attacker->>S: request cert giving B's honest encryption key
        S-->>Attacker: certB = sign((B, pkB), skS)
        Attacker->>S: request cert giving sign((A, attacker_controlled_key), skS)
        S-->>Attacker: certA* = sign((A, attacker_controlled_key), skS)
        Note over Attacker,Resp: certA* is reused as responder cert1
        Attacker->>Resp: start with xB = B
        Attacker->>Resp: certA*, certB, encrypt(sign((A, B, k), attacker_secret), pkB)
        Resp-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretB, k)
        Note over Attacker: decrypts and learns secretB
    end
result.explanation: The trace shows that the attacker can publicly add encryption-key bindings for honest names A and B through process_encK. That lets the server issue signed certificates for attacker-chosen keys under honest identities. In the secretA trace, the attacker registers B with an attacker-owned public key, obtains a server-signed cert2 for B, starts the initiator as xA = B, and decrypts the returned session-key message to recover k and then secretA. In the secretB trace, the attacker gets a server-signed certificate on (A, attacker-controlled key) and reuses it as cert1 at the responder, because cert1 and cert2 are both just untagged signed pairs. The responder then accepts an attacker-forged session key and releases secretB. So the visible failure is that honest name-to-key bindings can be attacker-inserted via enc_keys, enabling forged certified keys and certificate confusion.
result.notes: The supplied trace shows secrecy failures for secretA and secretB, even though the comment block in the .pv file mentions only injective-authentication failures. Also, cert1/cert2 are structurally similar signed pairs, so adding explicit tags to certificate formats would be a useful hardening step, but it is not the minimal fix needed for the concrete traces shown.
